does: This paper introduces KAN-SAE, a variant of sparse autoencoders that replaces standard linear ReLU activations with learnable per-feature B-spline nonlinearities derived from Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks. Applied to Sonny, a deep learning weather prediction model, KAN-SAE discovers 975 interpretable features compared to 566 for linear baseline SAEs—a 72% improvement in feature density—while reducing inter-feature redundancy by 20% and maintaining comparable reconstruction quality. Notably, without any climate-specific supervision, the method automatically identifies physically meaningful weather phenomena including European heatwave patterns spatially concentrated over western Europe and western Pacific typhoon tracking features.
connects: This work builds on mechanistic interpretability research that has increasingly applied sparse autoencoders to understand neural network representations, extending the line of work using SAEs for transformer interpretability. It directly addresses a fundamental limitation of existing SAE approaches—their assumption of linear feature superposition—which proves inadequate for capturing the inherently nonlinear dynamics of atmospheric systems. The paper combines recent advances in Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks as a flexible basis for nonlinear transformations with the established SAE framework for discovering sparse, interpretable features in large models, responding to calls for better mechanistic understanding of deep learning weather models.
enables: This approach opens pathways for systematically reverse-engineering how weather models encode physical climate phenomena, potentially accelerating discovery of latent representations that correspond to real meteorological processes. The KAN-SAE framework generalizes beyond weather—it provides a principled method for discovering nonlinear feature decompositions in any domain where linear superposition assumptions fail, suggesting applications to fluid dynamics, climate modeling, and other physics-informed neural networks. Future work could leverage these interpretable features for model improvement, transfer learning, uncertainty quantification, and building more transparent hybrid physics-neural hybrid systems.
